key_departures.impact: Five-time Pro Bowl EDGE and unquestioned best player on the roster. Traded to Baltimore for 2026 first-round pick (No. 14 overall) and a 2027 first-round pick. Generational-level loss at the position. No single signing can replace his production.
Released by the team, clearing the way for presumed No. 1 overall pick Fernando Mendoza. Departure was planned and expected. QB need addressed via upcoming draft.

Released after one season in Las Vegas. Started at guard but was an underperformer. Departure clears path for Jackson Powers-Johnson at right guard and opens the center spot that Linderbaum now fills.
Raiders' all-time No. 2 scorer and eight-year veteran. Signing of Matt Gay signals Carlson will not return. Reliable kicker replaced by a more inconsistent option.
Backup QB departing to Carolina. Low-impact departure given franchise direction toward rookie QB Mendoza.
Rotational linebacker on a one-year deal. Not a high-priority player for Las Vegas. Departure is essentially a non-event given the team's plans to overhaul the LB room.

signings.analysis: Record-setting contract makes Linderbaum the highest-paid interior offensive lineman in NFL history. Three years, $81M with $60M guaranteed is a full commitment — this is unambiguously their guy at center for the next 3 years. The Raiders' OL was historically bad in 2025 (allowed 64 sacks, last in rushing yards per game). Linderbaum's 96.2% pass block win rate since entering the league in 2022 is elite. Also solidifies Jackson Powers-Johnson's role at right guard. IOL drops from a tier 1 critical need to a tier 3–4 depth need at guard only. A guard addition in the draft (Day 2–3) remains plausible but not urgent.

Three-year deal with $32M guaranteed is a real investment, but Paye is not a Crosby replacement — he's a solid starter who had a down year in 2025 (4 sacks) after posting 8.5 and 8 in prior seasons. His 30.5 career sacks in five seasons give him credibility. At $16M AAV he's mid-market for EDGE. With Koonce on a one-year deal alongside him, EDGE is partially addressed — they have a starting-caliber player but clearly need another edge rusher opposite Paye. ESPN noted 'Las Vegas will need to add another pass rusher to play on the other side of Paye.' EDGE drops from tier 1 to tier 2 — still a meaningful draft need, likely Day 1 or 2.

One-year, $11M deal is a prove-it contract. Koonce was second on the team in pressures behind Crosby in 2025 (38 pressures, 4.5 sacks) and has a relationship with DC Rob Leonard. The one-year structure signals the Raiders view him as a bridge/rotation piece, not a long-term answer. He fits the 3-4 scheme and provides depth alongside Paye, but does not resolve the need for a second starter-caliber EDGE. Minimally addresses the EDGE need.

Acquired via trade (Raiders sent 6th-round pick, received Johnson + 7th-round pick). Johnson has two years remaining on his existing contract at $8.1M base in 2026. Former second-team All-Pro in 2023, but has declined since. Used primarily as a slot/nickel cornerback. Adds legitimate depth and starting-caliber talent at CB alongside re-signed Eric Stokes, meaningfully improving a secondary that was one of the worst in the league. However, at 29 and coming off a down 2025 season, this does not fully resolve the CB need — CB remains a viable draft target especially on Day 2.
signings.trade_details: Traded 2026 6th-round pick to Buffalo Bills; received Johnson and 2026 7th-round pick

signings.analysis: Three-year re-sign with $20M guaranteed is a genuine commitment. Stokes started 16 games in 2025 — his best healthy season — finishing with 53 tackles and 5 pass breakups. He was a bright spot in an otherwise poor secondary. Pairing Stokes with Taron Johnson gives the Raiders a functional starting CB duo and stabilizes the position group. Together these two CB moves partially address the CB need. CB is no longer a tier 1 crisis but remains a viable Day 2–3 draft target given the lack of proven upside and the age/decline of Johnson.

Three-year, $36M with $20M guaranteed. Dean is 25 years old and one of the best blitzing linebackers in the NFL — a Super Bowl champion who had 4 sacks in just 10 games in 2025. His coverage ability directly addresses the Raiders' 2025 linebacker coverage failures. Combined with Quay Walker (see below), the Raiders have overhauled the linebacker room from one of the league's worst to a legitimate strength. LB is fully addressed — need drops from tier 1 to tier 4. No draft resources required at LB.

Three-year, $40.5M with $28M guaranteed. Walker is a former first-round pick (No. 22 overall, 2022) who had a career-high 128 tackles in 2025 and recorded 100+ tackles in each of his four NFL seasons. His 4.5% missed tackle rate directly solves the Raiders' 2025 tackling issues. At $13.5M AAV he ranks just behind Pittsburgh's Patrick Queen among the highest-paid ILBs. Together with Nakobe Dean, the Raiders now have two of the best linebackers from this free agent class. LB is fully addressed. The Raiders spent $76.5M combined on the position — this need is done.

Three-year, $35M with $23M guaranteed. Nailor totaled 57 catches for 858 yards and 10 touchdowns over the past two seasons with the Vikings — solid production for a WR2/slot role. He slots alongside Tre Tucker and Jack Bech, forming a young, sub-28 trio that can develop alongside presumed rookie QB Fernando Mendoza and All-Pro TE Brock Bowers. At $11.7M AAV he's mid-market WR money. Partially addresses WR need — they have reasonable WR depth now but no true No. 1 receiver. WR remains a viable Day 1–2 draft target given the likely reliance on a rookie QB.

Contract terms not fully disclosed at time of reporting; estimated based on market rate. Gay is a seven-year veteran who made the Pro Bowl in 2021 but was inconsistent in 2025 (17-of-23 FG, 73.9% with Washington before going 8-of-8 with SF). His arrival signals Daniel Carlson's departure after 8 seasons. Carlson was the superior kicker statistically. Gay is an upgrade in terms of youth and recency but a lateral-to-downgrade move in terms of projected accuracy. Minimally addresses the K position created by Carlson's expected departure.
ERFA tender. Snowden is a depth/rotational edge player retained at the minimum. Does not move the needle on the EDGE need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Geno Smith released, Kenny Pickett departed to Carolina. The Raiders are widely expected to use the No. 1 overall pick on Indiana QB Fernando Mendoza. QB remains the top overall need/pick but is addressed via the draft, not free agency. Tier remains 1 but is draft-solved, not FA-solved.

Trading Maxx Crosby escalated this from need to critical crisis, but the signings of Kwity Paye (3yr/$48M, $32M gtd) and Malcolm Koonce (1yr/$11M) provide a functional starting duo. Paye is a real investment but not a Crosby-tier player. ESPN noted the Raiders still need another edge rusher on the other side of Paye. EDGE drops from tier 1 to tier 2 — strong Day 1 or early Day 2 draft target.

Tyler Linderbaum signing (3yr/$81M, $60M gtd, record IOL contract) is a slam dunk that fully resolves center. Alex Cappa released clears the path for Jackson Powers-Johnson at RG. Guard depth/development remains a minor consideration. IOL drops from tier 1 to tier 3 — guard via Day 2-3 draft pick is reasonable but not urgent.

Raiders signed both Nakobe Dean (3yr/$36M, $20M gtd) and Quay Walker (3yr/$40.5M, $28M gtd) — two of the best linebackers on the market. Combined $76.5M total value. Both are 25 years old, both are starter-caliber, both multi-year commitments. LB drops from tier 1 critical need to tier 4 — no draft capital needed here.

Re-signing Stokes (3yr/$30M, $20M gtd) and trading for Johnson (contract through 2027, $8.1M base) gives the Raiders a functional starting CB pair. Secondary is no longer a crisis. However, Johnson is 29 and declining; neither player profiles as a true No. 1 CB. CB drops from tier 1 to tier 2 — still a quality Day 2 draft target.

Jalen Nailor signing (3yr/$35M, $23M gtd) adds a legitimate WR2 option and improves depth alongside Tucker and Bech. However, with a rookie QB incoming, the Raiders lack a proven No. 1 WR. Nailor did not move the needle enough to drop the need tier — WR remains a tier 2 priority for the draft, likely Day 1 or 2.
No OT moves made in free agency. Kolton Miller expected back from injury. Miller + Linderbaum provides an anchor on the left side but right tackle remains a need. OT unchanged at tier 2.
No IDL signings confirmed. The Raiders' interior defensive line was below average in 2025. With the move to a 3-4 base under DC Rob Leonard, a capable NT is crucial. IDL unchanged at tier 2.
No safety signings confirmed. The Raiders lost Tre'von Moehrig (previous year) and the safety room was a weakness. ERFA Tristin McCollum tendered but is depth only. S unchanged at tier 2.

key_additions.headline: Record-setting center deal ($81M) makes Linderbaum highest-paid interior lineman in NFL history — anchors a rebuild of the worst OL in the league
First-round LB with 100+ tackles in each of 4 NFL seasons joins overhauled Raiders linebacker room
Super Bowl champion and elite blitzing LB reunites with former Georgia teammate Walker to transform Raiders' worst position group
30.5-career-sack EDGE becomes lead pass rusher after Maxx Crosby trade — a partial but real answer to the biggest hole on the roster
Young WR with 10 TDs over past two seasons slots into Raiders' receiver room alongside Tucker and Bech ahead of rookie QB era
Former second-team All-Pro nickel CB acquired for a 6th-round pick — secondary depth alongside re-signed Eric Stokes
Breakout CB2 locked up on 3-year deal after career-best 16-start season — brings stability to Raiders secondary
Rotation EDGE retained on prove-it deal — 4.5 sacks and 38 pressures in 2025 despite returning from knee injury
Seven-year veteran kicker replaces franchise fixture Daniel Carlson after 8 seasons in Las Vegas

key_losses.headline: Five-time Pro Bowl EDGE and face of the franchise traded for two first-round picks — franchise-reshaping move that jumpstarts the rebuild
Starting QB released to clear the path for presumed No. 1 overall pick Fernando Mendoza
Underperforming guard released after one season, accelerating the Raiders' OL overhaul now anchored by Linderbaum
Raiders' all-time No. 2 scorer and 8-year kicker expected to depart following signing of Matt Gay
Backup QB departs to Carolina — minimal impact given Raiders' pivot to rookie starter
Rotational LB returns to his original team — low-impact departure as Raiders overhaul the entire LB corps

draft_capital_notes: Raiders hold the 2026 No. 1 overall pick (expected: QB Fernando Mendoza). Received No. 14 overall (2026) and a 2027 first-round pick from the Ravens in the Maxx Crosby trade. Also hold compensatory picks at No. 134 (4th round) and No. 175 (5th round). Sent a 2026 6th-round pick to Buffalo in the Taron Johnson trade. Net draft capital is very strong entering the 2026 draft.
